title: Redpanda Cross-Cloud Demo A — Leader Pinning + Fallthrough

options.content:
**Redpanda Cross-Cloud Demo A — single-pane view of all 5 brokers across rp-aws / rp-gcp / rp-azure**

Each panel queries all three clouds' Prometheus instances (datasources `Prometheus-AWS`, `Prometheus-GCP`, `Prometheus-Azure`) and aggregates the result by broker pod name. The pod naming convention `redpanda-rp-<cloud>-N` makes the cloud obvious in the legend.

**Why these panels show 'leader activity', not 'leader count':** Redpanda doesn't expose a per-broker leader-count gauge in `public_metrics`. The visible leader signal is the **produce traffic** each broker handles — only the partition leader receives produce requests, so a broker's produce rate (requests/sec or bytes-in/sec) is a direct, real-time proxy for how many leaders it is currently serving.

**Survivability when a cloud goes down:** if AWS is cordoned during Demo A step 3, the rp-aws Prometheus becomes unreachable and its series go to *No data* in each panel — but the GCP and Azure series keep streaming. You see the failover in real time without losing observability of the survivors. (For maximum resilience, also keep the per-cloud Grafana tabs open — if rp-gcp itself goes down, only the rp-aws / rp-azure Grafanas keep working.)

**Expected pattern during Demo A:**
- T0 (steady state): leaders pinned to AWS rack via `ordered_racks:aws,gcp,azure`. AWS pods (`redpanda-rp-aws-0/1`) carry all the produce traffic for `load-test`; GCP and Azure pods at 0 produce rate.
- After cordon: AWS series disappear (Prometheus-AWS unreachable). GCP pods (`redpanda-rp-gcp-0/1`) pick up the produce traffic within 5–10s.
- After uncordon: AWS series return. Produce traffic flows back to AWS rack (under sustained OMB load you may need the `transfer_leadership` admin API workaround documented in the README to push it back).

panels.title: Produce request rate per broker — ALL CLOUDS (leader-activity proxy)
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_kafka_request_latency_seconds_count{redpanda_request="produce"}[1m])). Only the partition leader receives produce requests, so a broker's produce rate is a direct proxy for how many leaders it is currently serving live traffic for. Redpanda doesn't expose a leader-count gauge directly — this is the closest non-derived signal. (`redpanda_kafka_max_offset` is per-replica, not per-leader, so a count() over it just reports replicas-held-per-broker, which is roughly equal across all brokers and not Demo-A-meaningful.)

panels.title: Bytes-in rate for 'load-test' per broker — ALL CLOUDS (leader-activity proxy)
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_kafka_request_bytes_total{redpanda_request="produce"}[1m])) — bytes the broker is serving as the leader for the OMB workload's partitions. When AWS is cordoned, AWS pods stop receiving produce traffic and their lines drop to 0; the GCP pods take over.

panels.title: Kafka throughput per broker — ALL CLOUDS (Bps)
panels.description: rate(redpanda_kafka_request_bytes_total[1m]). Stays roughly constant across the demo; brief notch during leader transitions.

panels.title: Leadership transfer rate per broker — ALL CLOUDS
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_raft_leadership_changes_total[1m])). Spikes when leader-balancer moves leaders during cordon and restore.
